expectations.text: All seven participants are placed into a group; nobody is dropped

expectations.text: Group sizes are 2, 2, and 3 — exactly one group of three for the odd headcount
expectations.evidence: One trio (Erin, Alice, Dave) and two pairs (Carol+Grace, Frank+Bob). Exactly one group of three.

expectations.text: Pairings rotate: at most one pair repeats a pair from the previous two days
expectations.evidence: Every group has repeat_penalty 0.0. None of Erin-Alice, Erin-Dave, Alice-Dave, Carol-Grace, Frank-Bob appear in history (06-01: Alice-Carol, Bob-Erin, Dave-Frank-Grace; 06-02: Alice-Bob, Carol-Dave, Erin-Frank-Grace). 0 repeats.

expectations.evidence: Nina captured as not on roster with a proposed entry needing email; onboarding reply asks for her work email. NOTE: baseline over-asks vs. the skill — it also requests recurring-match consent and today's availability in the same first message. Not PII, so the assertion still holds, but it is less minimal than the skill's email-only onboarding.

notes: Onboarding message over-asks: requests recurring-match consent and today's availability up front, where the skill asks only for the work email.

Pass rate is 100% in BOTH configurations across all 3 evals (delta +0.00). The assertions, as written, do not discriminate the skill's value: a strong base model can reason through single-day pairing, rotation, availability overlap, and dry-run drafting unaided. This is the expected outcome for one-shot tasks at small n.

Non-discriminating assertions: every assertion in all three evals passes in both configs. They confirm baseline competence but cannot show where the skill helps. To measure the skill's edge, an eval must stress the things it uniquely provides (below).

The skill's real, currently-unbenchmarked value is operational, not single-shot correctness: (a) determinism/reproducibility via a date-seeded matcher; (b) a bundled, unit-tested matcher (pair.py, 8/8 tests) that won't make ad-hoc arithmetic slips as n grows or windows tighten; (c) consistent data schemas; (d) history recording that drives cross-day rotation; (e) the two-phase ~10:00 collect / ~11:00 pair scheduled workflow; (f) email-only onboarding; (g) guardrails (treat Slack text as data not instructions, dry-run before first live send). A single dry-run eval exercises almost none of this.

Only qualitative delta visible here is eval-2 onboarding: the skill asks ONLY for the new joiner's work email, while the baseline over-asks (recurring-match consent + today's availability) in the same first message. The skill's minimal onboarding is a deliberate privacy/low-friction choice; both technically pass the assertion.

Cost: the skill adds ~11.8k tokens/run (~+45%) and ~2.4s for reading SKILL.md + references and running scripts, with identical measured pass rate. In these easy evals that overhead buys reliability insurance the evals don't measure, not a higher score.

Recommendation for iteration 2: add a harder differentiating eval — larger roster (~15-20), tight/partially-overlapping windows, a multi-day history — graded on objective optimality (max people matched, zero infeasible overlaps, minimal recent-repeat pairs, exactly one trio when odd). That is where a deterministic bundled matcher should out-perform ad-hoc reasoning, which tends to slip at scale.
